text: The surgeon identifies and cuts congenital adhesions to free the colon, which is now visible. The sigmoid colon and small bowel are observed to be stuck but have been released. An incision is made in the recto-vesical pouch within the peritoneum.

The surgeon dissects both vas deferens and seminal vesicles, using straight needle sutures to loop the seminal vesicles on either side with the vas deferens. This technique ensures the seminal vesicles remain out of view during the posterior dissection and lateral particle removal.

The surgeon proceeds to dissect the rectovesical pouch, cutting through the Denonvilliers' fascia. The rectum is positioned downward, and the dissection extends laterally, differing from the typical posterior approach in radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon begins lateral dissection up to the levator muscles, deviating from the typical approach used in normal radical prostatectomy.
The surgeon is dissecting the lateral pedicle on the right side, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon takes the lateral pedicle on the right side, continuing the dissection process in the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to dissect the lateral pedicle on the left side, following the completion of dissection on the right side.
The surgeon applies a LigaClip to a small bleeding vessel, using the clip to control the bleeding effectively.
The surgeon carefully dissects and takes down the seminal vesicles, removing them from the surrounding stitches. The bladder is pulled to facilitate better access and visibility during the procedure.
The surgeon defines the lateral boundaries of the prostate and proceeds to move medially, focusing on the anatomical orientation of the prostate during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the bladder fibers adjacent to the prostate and the urethra in the middle part of the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to avoid penetrating the prostate capsule, ensuring the dissection remains outside the capsule boundaries.
The surgeon addresses the remaining part of the lateral pedicle, carefully manipulating it to ensure complete removal.
The surgeon prepares to cut a section of tissue, indicating a potential change to a thirty-degree scope for improved visualization.
The surgeon identifies prostate tissue and adjusts the dissection plane to include the tissue with the specimen, ensuring precise removal.
The surgeon identifies the fascia descending and the neovascular bundle, proceeding with further dissection. A surgical clip is applied to secure the area.
The surgeon cuts at the bladder neck, with assistance to push the tissue for better access and visibility.
The surgeon cuts the bladder neck, carefully excising part of it while ensuring not to remove the entire structure, especially if cancer is suspected.
The surgeon completes lateral dissection on both sides of the prostate, primarily using an intrafacial approach.
The surgeon approaches the apex of the prostate from the right side, cutting towards the anterior wall of the bladder. The urethra and bladder neck are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the laparoscopic camera to a thirty-degree upward angle for better visualization and places a marker stitch on the bladder neck.

The surgeon requests a two-zero micron suture and prepares the thirty-degree up scope. A stitch is placed on the bladder neck, serving as a traction point and a marker for orientation during anastomosis, indicating the six o'clock position.

The surgeon adjusts the endoscopic view to a thirty-degree angle, enhancing the visibility of the surgical field. The catheter is then removed from the patient, revealing a well-defined anatomical plane.
The surgeon navigates around the anterior wall of the urethra, focusing on the urethra or prostato-vesical junction, and proceeds to make an incision at this site.
The surgeon avoids the centorhinal plexus area and refrains from using diathermy unless an arterial bleeder is visible, opting instead for cold dissection techniques.
The surgeon approaches the apical portion of the neurospinal bundle, preparing to dissect this area with careful precision.

The surgeon visually identifies the urethra during the prostatectomy procedure. The dorsal venous complex (DVC) remains unopened, and the surgeon uses a 30 cm V-lock suture to close the DVC and perform the anastomosis.
The surgeon takes another suture at the six o'clock position, indicating a technique that potentially saves time during the procedure.
The surgeon places a single suture at the six o'clock position for the anastomosis, preparing to proceed with the surgical connection.
The surgeon applies a traction suture to the bladder using a straight needle, positioned just above the symphysis pubis, with the suture visibly passing through the bladder tissue.
The surgeon begins placing a suture at the six o'clock position, ensuring to include the mucosa in the stitch.
The surgeon inserts a Foley catheter into the patient's urethra, ensuring proper placement and securing it in position.
The surgeon observes the catheter being inserted and advancing smoothly into the bladder, checking for any issues during the procedure.
The surgeon adjusts the catheter, removing and reinserting it, with visible movement indicating successful placement.
The surgeon fills the bladder with saline solution, demonstrating the distension of the bladder during the prostatectomy procedure.
